Class companion

Harness Engineering and Development Automation

Learn how repeated development work becomes reliable: first as a checklist, then as a script, then as a harness your team can trust.

Start the model Open worksheet
Definitions Automation ladder Harness model Examples Worksheet

The big idea

Automation runs the task. A harness makes the task trustworthy.

Development automation is the larger discipline. Harness engineering is one concrete craft inside it: designing the controlled wrapper around repeated work.

Development automation

Making repeated work executable.

Development automation turns repeated engineering work into commands, checks, gates, and workflows that a team can run consistently.

Repeatable Observable Safe
Harness engineering

Building the wrapper that controls the run.

A harness sets up the state, runs the target process, captures evidence, decides pass or fail, and leaves the developer with a useful result.

Controlled Verifiable Reusable

From memory to workflow

The automation ladder

A team usually climbs this ladder one rung at a time. The higher you go, the less the work depends on memory and the more it depends on evidence.

Checklist

The steps are written down, but a person still has to run and judge them.

Script

The steps become executable, but success may still be unclear.

Harness

The run is controlled, observed, and judged with a clear result.

Gate

The harness becomes a requirement before merge, release, or handoff.

Workflow

Several gates connect into CI, release, demo, or agent workflows.

Harness anatomy

Five jobs every useful harness performs

Use this model whenever you are designing a test harness, build harness, deployment harness, evaluation harness, or demo harness.

Setup

Create the conditions.

Prepare dependencies, files, environment variables, test data, ports, services, or fixtures.

Run

Execute the target.

Start the process, script, test suite, migration, app, model, workflow, or command.

Observe

Capture evidence.

Collect logs, screenshots, exit codes, reports, traces, metrics, generated files, or API responses.

Judge

Decide pass or fail.

Define the rule that tells the team whether the run succeeded, failed, or needs review.

Recover

Clean up or report.

Stop services, reset state, preserve useful artifacts, and explain what to do next.

Where harnesses appear

Examples students should recognize

The same harness pattern shows up in tiny local scripts and in full production pipelines. The scale changes. The job stays familiar.

npm test

Test harness

Sets up test fixtures, runs the suite, captures failures, and exits with a status CI can understand.

npm run verify

Pull request harness

Runs formatting, linting, unit tests, build checks, and smoke tests before review.

make demo

Demo harness

Starts the right services, loads demo data, opens the app, and keeps reset steps close.

just eval

AI eval harness

Runs prompts or agents against cases, records outputs, scores behavior, and stores evidence.

make migrate-check

Migration harness

Tests schema or data changes against isolated state before anything touches production.

pnpm preview

Release harness

Builds the app, serves the output, checks the route, and gives the team a preview target.

Golden paths

The goal is not to automate everything. The goal is to make the right thing easy to run correctly.

A golden path is the command the team can trust. It hides unnecessary ceremony, but it does not hide evidence. When it fails, it tells you where to look.

See command patterns

Trust rules

What makes development automation worth trusting?

The best automation saves time without hiding risk. Use these checks before turning a script into a gate or a shared team workflow.

Repeatable

It can run more than once without creating confusing state.

Isolated

It avoids production data, real customers, and destructive defaults.

Observable

It captures enough evidence to debug a failed run.

Reversible

It has a cleanup, rollback, reset, or recovery path.

Bounded

It is clear what the automation is allowed to change.

Command design

Good golden paths sound boring on purpose

A boring command name is a gift to the team. Nobody has to remember a long checklist when the project has one obvious command for the job.

npm run verify

Before review

Format, lint, test, build, and smoke check the change before a pull request.

make reset-demo

Before a live demo

Reset sample data, start the app, and confirm the route opens cleanly.

just release-check

Before shipping

Build output, run route checks, confirm assets, and save proof for release.

Class activity

Design a harness for one repeated workflow

Pick a workflow that already annoys you. The exercise is not to write code first. The exercise is to design trust first.

Fill this in during class

  • WorkflowWhat repeated task do you want to make reliable?
  • Current stepsWhat do people currently do by memory or checklist?
  • SetupWhat state, tools, services, files, or data must exist first?
  • RunWhat command, process, model, app, or workflow should execute?
  • EvidenceWhat output should the harness capture?
  • Pass/fail ruleHow does it know the run succeeded?
  • RecoveryWhat should be cleaned up, reset, or preserved afterward?
  • RiskWhat could this automation damage, hide, or misunderstand?
  • Golden pathWhat should the one-command version be called?
Worksheet template 
Workflow:

Current manual steps:

Setup required:

Command or process to run:

Evidence to capture:

Pass/fail rule:

Cleanup or recovery:

One risk:

One safety improvement:

Golden path command name:

Keep this sentence

A harness is a trust machine for repeated development work.

When you know the setup, the run, the evidence, the judgment, and the recovery path, you can turn fragile manual work into automation a team can actually rely on.
